title.zh: 鹰落仙人掌之地，阿兹特克帝国的湖上都城
title.en: Where the Eagle Alighted on the Cactus: Island Capital of the Aztec Empire

summary.zh: 阿兹特克帝国的湖上都城，传统纪年建于1325年。维齐洛波奇特利以"鹰立石上仙人掌"为神示指定城址；这一图景至今在墨西哥国旗中央。
summary.en: Island capital of the Aztec empire, founded by tradition in 1325 where Huitzilopochtli's sign appeared — an eagle on a stone-borne cactus, the image at the center of the Mexican flag today.

description.zh:
特诺奇蒂特兰是墨西卡人在特斯科科湖岛上建立的都城，传统纪年系于1325年。据建城传说，部族守护神维齐洛波奇特利早有神谕：在鹰立于石上仙人掌之处定居。传说的前情是一场神族仇杀——战神之甥科皮尔来袭被杀，心脏被掷入湖中，落处长出仙人掌；流亡的墨西卡人穿行芦苇沼泽，终于望见神示的鹰，遂在无人肯要的湖沼之地夯土立庙。"特诺奇蒂特兰"意为"石上仙人掌果之地"。约1541年成书的《门多萨手抄本》扉页正是这一场景：鹰立于仙人掌，四周十位建城者环坐，蓝色对角线划出四个城区；鹰喙衔蛇的细节出自杜兰等较晚的记载，后来进入墨西哥国徽与国旗。

两个世纪间，这座湖城长成美洲最大的都市之一，西班牙人到来时人口据估计有二十万上下。城市以"奇南帕"浮田种植粮蔬，三条石堤长桥连接湖岸，双水道引来查普尔特佩克的泉水；城心圣区矗立着约45米高的大神庙，塔顶并立雨神特拉洛克与战神维齐洛波奇特利的双殿——雨水与战争，帝国的一体两面。1519年随科尔特斯入城的士兵贝尔纳尔·迪亚斯写道，士兵们望见湖上的白塔与石堤，疑心自己在做梦。

1521年8月13日，特诺奇蒂特兰在围城战末陷落，西班牙人在废墟上建起墨西哥城。1978年电力工人挖出科约尔绍基圆石，触发大神庙遗址的系统发掘；今天，遗址就露天躺在墨西哥城主教座堂旁，城市中心的位置七个世纪未曾移动。
description.en:
Tenochtitlan was the Mexica capital built on an island in Lake Texcoco, its founding fixed by tradition in the year 1325. The foundation legend turns on a promised sign from Huitzilopochtli: settle where an eagle stands on a cactus growing from a stone. Behind the sign lay a divine feud — Copil, the god's hostile nephew, had been slain and his heart flung into the lake, and from where it landed the cactus grew. Wading through the reed marshes that no other people wanted, the Mexica saw the eagle and raised their first earthen shrine on the spot. The name means Place of the Stone-Cactus Fruit. The frontispiece of the Codex Mendoza (c. 1541) paints the scene: the eagle on the cactus, ten founders seated around it, blue diagonals dividing the city into four quarters. The serpent in the eagle's beak comes from later accounts such as Durán's, and passed from there onto the arms and flag of Mexico.

Within two centuries the lake city had grown into one of the largest in the Americas, with a population estimated around two hundred thousand at European contact. It fed itself from chinampa raised fields, reached the shore by three stone causeways, and drank spring water brought by a double aqueduct from Chapultepec. At its heart rose the Templo Mayor, some forty-five meters high, crowned by the twin shrines of Tlaloc and Huitzilopochtli — rain and war, the two faces of the empire. Bernal Díaz, marching in with Cortés in 1519, wrote that the soldiers, seeing the white towers rising from the water, wondered if they were dreaming.

The city fell on 13 August 1521 at the end of the great siege, and Mexico City was raised on its ruins. In 1978 electrical workers struck the Coyolxauhqui stone, setting off the systematic excavation of the Templo Mayor; the ruins now lie open to the sky beside the Metropolitan Cathedral, the center of the city unmoved in seven centuries.

value.en: An eagle on a cactus growing from a stone

sources.zh: 迭戈·杜兰《新西班牙印第安人史》（1581年）
sources.en: Diego Durán, Historia de las Indias de Nueva España (1581)

sources.zh: 贝尔纳尔·迪亚斯·德尔·卡斯蒂略《征服新西班牙信史》
sources.en: Bernal Díaz del Castillo, Historia verdadera de la conquista de la Nueva España

label.zh: 墨西哥城中心（特诺奇蒂特兰故址）
label.en: Historic center of Mexico City, site of Tenochtitlan
